senses.g: alternative form of دِہْلی (dihlī): Delhi (a megacity and union territory of India, containing the national capital New Delhi)

دم.e: From Sanskrit दम्भ (dambha). Cognate with Persian دم (dam).
Borrowed from Classical Persian دَم (dam), borrowed from Arabic دَم (dam), from Proto-Semitic *dam-, from Proto-Afroasiatic *dam-.

دماغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِمَاغ (dimāġ), borrowed from Arabic دِمَاغ (dimāḡ), a blend of دَم (dam) + مُخّ (muḵḵ).

senses.g: Damascus (the capital city of Syria; an ancient settlement, the ancient capital of various polities, most notably the Umayyad Caliphate from 661 to 744 CE and Aram-Damascus, existing from the 12th to 8th centuries BCE)

دمشق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دمشق (damišq), from Arabic دِمَشْق (dimašq).

ex.u:
صبح اٹھ کر میں نے ڈاکٹر کو بلوایا اور معائنہ کروایا۔
انھوں نے بھی اس امر کی تصدیق کی ہے کہ یہ دمہ ہے۔
ex.r:
subh uṭh kar ma͠i ne ḍākṭar ko bulvāyā aur mu'āina karvāyā.
unhõ ne bhī is amr kī tasdīq kī hai ki ye dama hai.
ex.e:
When I woke up in the morning, I called the doctor for an examination.
 He, too, confirmed that it is asthma.

دمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَمَه (dama). By surface analysis, دَم (dam, “breath”) + ہ (h).

senses.g: day; daytime (the period from sunrise till sunset)
luck; fate (ie. to have good or bad luck)

دن.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi دن (dn /⁠din⁠/) (c. 1503), from Prakrit 𑀤𑀺𑀡 (diṇa), from Sanskrit दिन (diná), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *dyew-. Cognates Cognates include Punjabi دِݨ (diṇ) / دِن (din), Saraiki دِݨ (diṇ) and Sindhi ڏِنُ (ḍ̠iṇu).

senses.g: the bark of the tree that is used to clean your teeth

دندان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَنْدَان (dandān). Doublet of دانت (dā̃t).

senses.g: existence, current life (as opposed to the hereafter)
society, all people, everyone (in this world)

دنیا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دُنْیَا (dunyā), borrowed from Arabic دُنْيَا (dunyā), from أَدْنَى (ʔadnā), from دَنِيّ (daniyy). First attested in c. 1435 as Middle Hindi دنیا (dnya).

senses.g: secular (ie. as opposed to religious or spiritual)
Danyor (a city in Gilgit district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)

دو.e: Inherited from Old Hindi दो (do), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑁄 (do), from Sanskrit द्व (dvá), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *dwáH, from Proto-Indo-European *dwóh₁.

senses.g: a portable case with receptacles for ink and writing-reeds
The style with which the cloth or wool in an (Eastern) inkbottle is stirred.

دوات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دوات (dawāt), from Arabic دَوَاة (dawāh). Compare Gujarati દવાત (davāt), Marathi दौत (daut), Punjabi ਦਵਾਤ (davāt) / دوات (doāt), Assamese দোৱাত (düat).

دوار.e: From Sanskrit द्वार (dvāra), from Proto-Indo-Iranian, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰworom (“enclosure, courtyard, i.e. something enclosed by the door”), from *dʰwor-, compare *dʰwer-.

دوالی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀤𑀻𑀯𑀸𑀮𑀻 (dīvālī), 𑀤𑀻𑀯𑀸𑀯𑀮𑀻 (dīvāvalī) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Sanskrit दीपावलि (dīpāvali), दीपावली (dīpāvalī). Doublet of دِیپاوَلِی (dīpāvalī).

دوائی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دوائی (dawā'ī), from Arabic دَوَاء (dawāʔ, “medicine”). By surface analysis, دوا (davā) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: again, anew, twice, second time, newly

دوبارہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دوباره (dōbāra, dubāra). Alternatively from دو (do) + بار (bār), both elements from Sanskrit.

دوپہر.e: From دو (do, “two”) + پَہَر (pahar, “measure of time equalling three hours”).

دودھ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi دودھ (dvdh /⁠dūdh⁠/) (c. 1411), / دود (dvd /⁠dūd⁠/) (c. 1635), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀼𑀤𑁆𑀥 (duddha), from Sanskrit दुग्ध (dugdhá), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *dʰugdʰás, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰugʰ-tó-s, from *dʰewgʰ- (“to yield”).

دور.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀽𑀭 (dūra), from Sanskrit दूर (dūrá). Also influenced by Classical Persian دور (dūr).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀽𑀭 (dūra), from Sanskrit दूर (dūrá). Also influenced by Classical Persian دور (dūr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian دَور (dawr), borrowed from Arabic دَوْر (dawr), from دَارَ (dāra).

دور گو.e: Learned borrowing from Classical Persian دور (dūr, “far”) + گفتن (guftan, “to speak”). Calque of English telephone. Compare دُورْبھَاش (dūrbhāś) (a learned borrowing from Sanskrit दूरभाष (dūrabhāṣa), compare Hindi दूरभाष (dūrbhāṣ)) and ٹیلی فون (ṭelī fon) (borrowed from English telephone).

senses.g: Doordarshan (the first television network of India)

ex.u: اَمْرِت سَر دُورْدَرْشَن نے جو بھارْتِی فِلْموں کِی ثَقافَتِی یَلْغار شُرُوع کِی ہَے اُس سے بَچْنے [کے]... کْیا اِنْتِظام کِئے ہَیں
ex.e: Amrit sir, what preparations have been made to survive Doordarshan's cultural onslaught of Indian films?

دوردرشن.e: Calque of English television: دُور (dūr, “far”) + دَرْشَن (darśan, “vision, sight”).
Calque of English television: دُور (dūr, “far”) + دَرْشَن (darśan, “vision, sight”).

دورلیکھ.e: دور (dūr, “far”) + لیکھ (lekh, “writing”), calque of English telegraph.

دوڑنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀯𑀤𑀺 (davadi) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-), from Sanskrit द्रवति (drávati). Cognate with Punjabi ਦੌੜਣਾ (dauṛṇā) / دَوڑنا (ḍauṛṇā), Sindhi ڊوڙَڻُ (ḍoṛaṇu) / डोड़णु (ḍoṛaṇu), Gujarati દોડવું (doḍvũ), Nepali दौडनु (dauḍanu), Marathi दौडणे (dauḍṇe), Assamese দৌৰা (doura).

senses.g: the stomach (especially one that is hungry), an empty stomach, belly
Nafs al-Ammara (the human inclination or ego towards sin)

دوزخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دوزَخ (dōzax). First attested as Old Hindi दोजग (dojaga).

دوست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دوسْت (dōst), from Middle Persian 𐭣𐭥𐭮𐭲𐭩 (dwst' /⁠dōst⁠/), from Old Persian 𐎭𐎢𐏁𐎫𐎠 (d-u-š-t-a /⁠dauštā⁠⁠/), from Proto-Iranian *jawštā́, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ȷ́awštā́, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵéws-tōr ~ *ǵus-tr-és, from *ǵews-.

دوستانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دوستانه (dōstāna). Compare Punjabi ਦੋਸਤਾਨਾ (dostānā) / دوستانہ (dostānh), Sindhi دوستاڻو (dostāṇo) / दोस्ताणो.
Borrowed from Classical Persian دوستانه (dōstāna). Compare Punjabi ਦੋਸਤਾਨਾ (dostānā) / دوستانہ (dostānh), Sindhi دوستاڻو (dostāṇo) / दोस्ताणो.

دوستی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دوسْتِی (dōstī). By surface analysis, دوسْت (dost) + ـی (-ī).

دوسرا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *द्विःसर (*dviḥsara, “two-stringed”), from द्विः (dviḥ, “two”) + सर (sara, “string, cord”) but with the first element following Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀼𑀯𑁂 (duve, “two”).
Inherited from Sanskrit *द्विःसर (*dviḥsara, “two-stringed”), from द्विः (dviḥ, “two”) + सर (sara, “string, cord”) but with the first element following Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀼𑀯𑁂 (duve, “two”).

senses.g: of mixed breed, race, or species

دوغلا.e: Corrupted from Classical Persian داغول (dāġūl, “illegitimate child; trickster, cheat”).

senses.g: an illegitimate child; bastard, get, love child

دوغلا.e: Corrupted from Classical Persian داغول (dāġūl, “illegitimate child; trickster, cheat”).

senses.g: Duki (a city in Duki district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

دولت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَولَت (dawlat), borrowed from Arabic دَوْلَة (dawla).

دولہا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀤𑀼𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀳 (dullaha), ultimately from Sanskrit दुर्लभक (durlabhaka, literally "hard to attain or gain"), from दुर्- (dur-, “hardly”) + लभक (labhaka, “attained”), itself a compound of the root लभ् (labh, “to gain, attain”) + -क (-ka, adjectival suffix from Proto-Indo-Iranian *-kas).

senses.g: inflection of دُولْہا (dūlhā, “bridegroom; groom”): — direct plural
inflection of دُولْہا (dūlhā, “bridegroom; groom”): — oblique/vocative singular

دونا.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa दूण (dūṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Prakrit 𑀤𑀼𑀉𑀡 (duuṇa), 𑀤𑀺𑀉𑀡 (diuṇa), from Sanskrit द्विगुण (dviguṇa).
Inherited from Apabhramsa दूण (dūṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Prakrit 𑀤𑀼𑀉𑀡 (duuṇa), 𑀤𑀺𑀉𑀡 (diuṇa), from Sanskrit द्विगुण (dviguṇa).

senses.g: Dönmeh, a group of Sabbatean crypto-Jews.
first-person singular masculine future indicative of دینا (dēnā)
first-person singular feminine future indicative of دینا (dēnā)
Dehradun (a city, the state capital of Uttarakhand, India)

دہقان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِهْقَان (dihqān, “peasant, farmer; villager”).

دہلوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِهْلَوِی (dihlawī)۔ By surface analysis, دِہْلی (dihlī, “Delhi”) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: a surname transferred from the place name

دہلوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِهْلَوِی (dihlawī)۔ By surface analysis, دِہْلی (dihlī, “Delhi”) + ـی (-ī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian دِهْلَوِی (dihlawī)۔ By surface analysis, دِہْلی (dihlī, “Delhi”) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: Delhi (a megacity and union territory of India, containing the national capital New Delhi)

دہلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دهلی (dihlī), of uncertain origin. See दिल्ली (dillī) for further details.

دہی.e: Inherited from Old Hindi दही (dahī), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀤𑀳𑀺 (dahi) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit दधि (dádhi). Cognate to Punjabi دَہِیں (dahīṉ), Saraiki ڈَہِی (ḍahī), Sindhi ڏهي (ḏhe) / ॾही (d̤ahī), Gujarati દહીં (dahī̃), Nepali दही (dahī), Bengali দই (doi), Odia ଦହି (dahi), ଦଇ (dai), Assamese দৈ (doi).

دہیز.e: Probably influenced by ديجا (dejā, daijā, “dowry”), from Sanskrit.

senses.g: a region in Saudi Arabia comprising of the Holy cities of Mecca and Medina

ex.u: دوسری جانب سعودی وزارت داخلہ نے مناسک حج کے آغاز سے چند گھنٹے پہلے ایک اعلان میں بتایا ہے کہ دیارِ مقدّسہ پہنچنے والے عازمین کی تعداد بیس لاکھ ہو چکی ہے۔
ex.e: On the other hand, the Saudi Interior Ministry has announced in an announcement a few hours before the start of the Hajj rituals that the number of pilgrims who have reached the Holy Land has reached two million.

senses.g: the Holy Land (a region in the Middle East, consisting of Palestine and Israel, where Biblical events are set)

دیار مقدسہ.e: From Classical Persian دیارِ مقدسه (diyār-i muqaddasa). By surface analysis, دَیَار (dayār, “land; region”) + مُقَدَّسَہ (muqaddasa, “Holy; scared”).

دید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِید (dīd), from the verb دِیدَن (dīdan, “to see; look”). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi دید (dyd /⁠dīd⁠/).

دیر آئے درست آئے.e: Likely a calque of Classical Persian دیر آید درست آید (dēr āyad durust āyad, literally “come late, come safe”) which is also present in Urdu as an adverb.

دیرگھ.e: From Sanskrit दीर्घ (dīrghá), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *drHgʰa-, from Proto-Indo-European *dlh₁gʰó- (“long”).

دیز.e: Contracted form of دہيز (dahez), from Classical Persian جهیز (jahīz), from Arabic جهاز (jahāz, jihāz).

senses.g: one of the five deepak rags

دیس.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi دیس (des) (c. 1565), a semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit देश (dēśá), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *dayśás, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *dayćás, from Proto-Indo-European *deyḱós (“part, point, country”).

senses.g: local, native, indigenous (to the Indian subcontinent)
to expect, see, anticipate (ie. to see (something in the future))

دیکھنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀤𑁂𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀇 (dĕkkhaï). Urdu verb set دِکْھنا (dikhnā) دیکْھنا (dekhnā) دِکھانا (dikhānā) دِکھوانا (dikhvānā)

senses.g: inflection of دیکھنا: — masculine plural infinitive participle

دین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِین (dīn), from Middle Persian dyn' (dēn).

دین دار.e: A compound of دین (dīn) and the suffix ـدار (-dār).

دینا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi देना (denā), from Prakrit 𑀤𑁂 (de) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix), the first element of which is from Sanskrit दा (dā́).

دینی.e: From Arabic دِينِيّ (dīniyy) (through Classical Persian دینی (dīnī)). By surface analysis, دین (dīn) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: inflection of دینا (dēnā): — masculine plural infinitive participle

دیوار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دیوار (dīwār), from Middle Persian dywʾl (dēwār). Cognate to Punjabi دیوار (dīvār).

دیوار گریہ.e: Calque of English Wailing Wall. From دِیْوَار (dīvār, “wall”) + ِ (i, “izafat construction, of”) + گِرْیَہ (girya, “weeping”).

دیوانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دیوانه (dēwāna). Compare Punjabi ਦੀਵਾਨਾ (dīvānā) / دِیوانَہ (dīvānah), Bengali দেওয়ানা (deōẇana).
Borrowed from Classical Persian دیوانه (dēwāna). Compare Punjabi ਦੀਵਾਨਾ (dīvānā) / دِیوانَہ (dīvānah), Bengali দেওয়ানা (deōẇana).

دیوبندی.e: From Deoband, town in Uttar Pradesh, India, the location of the Islamic seminary in which the movement began.

senses.g: a man who panders his own wife
a generic term of abuse for a man

دیوث.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَیُّوث (dayyūs), from Arabic دَيُّوث (dayyūṯ).

دیور.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi دیور (dyvr) (c. 1647), from Prakrit 𑀤𑁂𑀯𑀭 (devara), from Sanskrit देवर (devará), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *daHiwará-, from Proto-Indo-European *dayh₂wḗr, with retention of -v- influenced by the Sanskrit term. Far cognate with Russian де́верь (déverʹ) and Lithuanian dieveris.

senses.g: wife of husband's younger brother (sister-in-law) – feminine of دیوَر (devar)

دیورانی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀤𑁂𑀯𑀭𑀸𑀡𑀻 (devarāṇī), from Sanskrit देवरजानी (devarajānī).

senses.g: direct plural of دیوْرَانی (devrānī, “wife of husband's younger brother (sister-in-law)”)
vocative plural of دیوْرَانی (devrānī, “wife of husband's younger brother (sister-in-law)”)
oblique plural of دیوْرَانی (devrānī, “wife of husband's younger brother (sister-in-law)”)
vocative plural of دیوَر (devar, “husband's younger brother (brother-in-law)”)
oblique plural of دیوَر (devar, “husband's younger brother (brother-in-law)”)

دیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ده (dih) or from variant دیه (dīh).

senses.g: Dhadar (a city in Kachhi district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

دھاگا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀥𑀸𑀕𑁆𑀕 (*dhāgga). Cognate with Punjabi ਧਾੱਗਾ (dhāggā) / دھاگا (dhāggā), Sindhi ڌاڳو (dhāg̠o), and Gujarati ધાગો (dhāgo).

دھاندل.e: Perhaps from Sanskrit द्वंद्व (dvaṃdva). Compare Marathi धांदल (dhāndal).

ex.u: اس الیکشن میں دھاندلی ہوئی ہے!
ex.r: is ilaikśan mē̃ dhāndlī hū'ī hai!

دھاندلی.e: From دھاندل (dhāndal, “rigging”) + ی (ī). Compare Marathi धांदली (dhāndlī).

دھرتی.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa धरित्ती (dharittī) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Sanskrit धरित्री (dharitrī).

senses.g: lawfully wedded wife (literally, “married in accordance to one's dharma”)

دھڑکن.e: Related to دَھڑَکنا (dhaṛaknā, “to beat, throb”), from Prakrit *𑀥𑀟𑁆 (*dhaḍ, “beat”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-).

دھکا.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀥𑀓𑁆𑀓 (*dhakka), from *𑀥𑀓𑁆𑀓𑁆 (*dhakk). Compare Gujarati ધક્કો (dhakko), Bengali ধাক্কা (dhakka).

senses.g: running through fire (a practice of fakirs or qalandars)
furore; a wave of enthusiastic admiration

دھن.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀥𑀼𑀡𑀺 (dhuṇi), from Sanskrit धुनि (dhúni, “sounding, noisy”, adjective).

دھند.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi دھند (dhnd /⁠dhund⁠/) (c. 1562), from Sanskrit *धूमान्ध (*dhūmāndha), from धूम (dhūmá, “smoke, haze”) + अन्ध (andhá, “blind”) (compare धूमाक्ष (dhūmākṣá)).

senses.g: occupation; business; work (especially one that is dubious or immoral)

دھندا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀥𑀁𑀥 (dhaṃdha), from Sanskrit धन्ध (dhandha). Cognate to Gujarati ધંધો (dhandho), Marathi धंदा (dhandā), Bengali ধাঁধা (dhãdha).

دھنیہ واد.e: From Sanskrit धन्य (dhanya, “auspicious, blessed, lucky”) + वाद (vāda, “speech”).
From Sanskrit धन्य (dhanya, “auspicious, blessed, lucky”) + वाद (vāda, “speech”).

دھواں.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀥𑀽𑀫 (dhūma), from Sanskrit धूम (dhūma), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *dʰuHmás, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰuh₂mós, from *dʰewh₂- + *-mós. First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi دھواں (dhvaṉ /⁠dhūā̃⁠/).

senses.g: sunlight, sunshine; heat of the Sun

دھوپ.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀥𑀼𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀸 (*dhuppā). Cognate Punjabi دُھپّ (dhupp) / ਧੁੱਪ (dhuppa) and Gujarati ધૂપ (dhūp).

senses.g: grey (of a color between black and white)

دھوسر.e: From Sanskrit धूसर (dhūsara), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *dʰewh₂-. Related to Hindi धुआँ (dhuā̃)
From Sanskrit धूसर (dhūsara), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *dʰewh₂-. Related to Hindi धुआँ (dhuā̃)

دھوم.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *धुन्मन् (*dhunman). Related to Sanskrit धुनिमन्त् (dhunimant, “noisy”).

senses.g: a male given name from Sanskrit, equivalent to English Dhurandhar

دھونے والی مشین.e: دھونے (dhone, “washing”) + والی (vālī, “one, whose purpose is”) + مشین (maśīn, “machine”).

دھیان.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit ध्यान (dhyāna). Doublet of دِین (dīn).

senses.g: The twelfth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by د and followed by ذ.

ڈاکا.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀟𑀸𑀓𑁆𑀓 (*ḍākka) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), of uncertain origin; see there for more.

ex.u: آج صبح ڈاکیے نے ہمارے گھر ڈاک ڈالی
ex.r: āj subah ḍākiyah ne hamārā ghar ḍāk ḍālī
ex.e: The postman delivered the mail to our home today.

senses.g: an auxiliary verb: indicates speed or recklessness

ڈالنا.e: Urdu verb set ڈلنا ڈالنا ڈلوانا (ḍlvānā) Etymology tree Proto-Indo-European *der- Sanskrit दॄ (dṝ) Sanskrit दारयति (dārayati) Sanskrit दालयति (dālayati)? Proto-Indo-European *dʰwerh₁-? Sanskrit ध्वृ (dhvṛ) Sanskrit ध्वर॑ति (dhvárati)? Prakrit 𑀠𑀮𑀇 (ḍhalaï)? Prakrit *𑀟𑀸𑀮𑁂𑀇 (*ḍālei) Urdu ڈالنا Inherited from Prakrit *𑀟𑀸𑀮𑁂𑀇 (*ḍālei). Cognate with Marathi डाळणे (ḍāḷṇe, “to pile, stack”), Assamese ঢলা (dhola).

ڈر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀟𑀭 (ḍara), 𑀤𑀭 (dara), from Sanskrit दर (dara).

senses.g: a nightmare – clipping of ڈَراؤنی خْواب (ḍarāonī xvāb)

ڈرپوک.e: From the stems of ڈرنا (“to be afraid”) + پونکنا (“to be fear-stricken”).

ڈرنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀟𑀭𑀇 (ḍaraï), from Sanskrit दरति (darati).

senses.g: a surname from English, equivalent to English Disney
Daska (a city in Sialkot district, Punjab, Pakistan)

ڈسکہ.e: From Urdu دس (“ten”) +Urdu کہ (“koh, a Mughal unit of measurement”).

senses.g: the roar of a lion, tiger (and by extension) elephant, camel

ڈکار.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀟𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸𑀭 (*ḍakkāra), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀟𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸𑀭 (*ḍakkāra). Cognate with Punjabi ਡਕਾਰ (ḍakār) / ڈکار (ḍakār), Nepali डकार (ḍakār), Marathi ढेकर (ḍhekar), डकार (ḍakār), Kashmiri ڈاکُر (ḍākur).

ڈکارنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀟𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸𑀭𑀇 (*ḍakkāraï, “to belch”), from Prakrit *𑀟𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸𑀭 (*ḍakkāra, “burp, belch”). Related to ڈَکار (ḍakār, “burp”). Urdu verb set ڈَکَرْنا (ḍakarnā) ڈَکْرانا (ḍakrānā) ڈَکارْنا (ḍakārnā) ڈاکارْنا (ḍākārnā)

senses.g: to shiver (ie. due to an illness)
Dambudas (a city in Roundu district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)

ڈمرو.e: Inherited from Sanskrit डमरु (ḍamaru), from डमरु (ḍamaru) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-).

ڈنڈا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀟𑀁𑀟𑀕 (ḍaṃḍaga), from Sanskrit दण्डक (daṇḍaka), from दण्ड (daṇḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-). Cognate with Hindi डाँड़ (ḍā̃ṛ) and दंड (daṇḍ), Bengali ডান্ডা (ḍanḍa), and Punjabi ਡੰਡਾ (ḍaṇḍā).

senses.g: Dunkirk (a town in Nord department, Hauts-de-France, France)

ex.u: دوسری جنگ عظیم کے دوران ڈنکرک سے اتحادی افواج کا انخلا ایک اہم اور قابل ذکر لمحہ تھا، جو زیادہ مشہور نہیں ہے۔ اسی انخلا کی کہانی میں تقریباً 300 ہندوستانی فوجیوں کے ایک دستے کا واقعہ بھی شامل ہے۔
ex.r: dūsrī jaṉg-e-ʻaz̤īm ke daurān ḍaṉkark se ittiḥādī afvāj kā inxilā aik aham aur qābil-e ẕikr lamḥa thā, jo ziyada maśhūr nahīṉ hai. isī inxilā kī kahānī meṉ taqreban 300 hindūstānī faujiyoṉ ke aik daste kā vāqiʻa bhī śāmil hai.
ex.e: The withdrawal of Allied forces from Dunkirk during World War II was an important and notable moment, which is not well known. The story of the same withdrawal also includes the incident of a contingent of about 300 Indian soldiers.

ڈنکرک.e: Borrowed from English Dunkirk, derived from French Dunkerque.

senses.g: to illegally cross an international border
Dover (a town and major port in Kent, England)
Daharki (a city in Ghotki district, Sindh, Pakistan)
